text: - People ask the question,
- We're face-to-face as we walk through our cities
with the homeless crisis, child welfare crisis,
incarceration, poverty.
- You know, we've kind of lost hope in humanity.
- This is Dominic and Jedidiah—
and they haven't given up hope.
They see effective nonprofits at work in all U.S. cities,
but many seem to be acting alone
without access to the resources
or the volunteers needed to make a citywide transformation.
Their new idea:
Form an unlikely partnership
with the 235 million Americans in the faith community.
- The core of the faith community
is to love the neighbor
or to activate generosity at scale.
Oftentimes, our faith communities are disconnected.
They're not united around
addressing the greatest issues facing our cities.
But we believe people want to unite—
they just don't have the playbook,
and they don't have the roadmap.
- Together, they formed "Love Has No Limits,"
an organization that would unite faith groups
to serve alongside nonprofits.
Their first step was a massive experiment in Los Angeles.
- There's incredible human beings in all these cities
that for years or decades
have been trying to make a difference—
and they've been doing it alone
and they've been doing it isolated.
And these are the moments that say,
"Look, there's actually an army of us!
And if we could unite the power of collective might,
what's really possible?”
- It won't be easy, but if they can pull it off,
could this new model be the way to actually solve
some of the greatest challenges facing America?
- The United States has spent
over $16 trillion in the last 60 years
on poverty-related programs
and has seen almost no results.
So, what's different about
the approach these guys are taking?
- You know, we believe in people.
We believe that people closest to the problem
are best positioned to solve it.
And so when you think about
actually empowering an individual
to serve their community and solve its problems,
government being top-down,
unfortunately, is just so disconnected.
And there's so many relational layers that are missing
that it can actually never really serve
and solve that problem well.
- Of all the institutions best positioned
to bring bottom-up empowerment,
it is the faith community.
- The faith communities in America outgave
the government of America— to developing nations
in the past year—
outgave them by 40%.
So that the action of generosity is there,
the challenge is they often
just don't know how to work together,
and they really don't know where
they can enter into the conversation.
- Here's how it works:
The test of their strategy started in 2019.
Dominic and Jedidiah first held group meetings
with the nonprofits throughout L.A.
They identified the poverty, child welfare,
and city restoration projects they would tackle
if they had additional support.
Then, Love Has No Limits approached Los Angeles
faith groups, outlining the concept
and providing training for volunteers.
In their playbook,
houses of worship would become service centers
to collaborate with the local nonprofits.
- The way we're uniquely able
to come into a community or a city and bring diverse spheres
of influence together is we function as a platform of trust.
We're not trying to be the hope,
we're not trying to be the hero.
We're actually trying to be the backend support
to bringing all of these groups together.
- After a year
of behind-the-scenes collaboration,
Love Has No Limits brought everyone together
in a massive campaign called “1DayLA,”
the largest collaboration
of volunteer service Los Angeles had ever seen.
Over 20,000 volunteers worked
alongside local nonprofits and businesses.
- There's almost like this wind
of energy that says, "I'm not alone."
We have people in L.A. that were a block away
from each other that didn't know each other;
two different diverse organizations,
maybe didn't value each other,
and now they're working together in their communities
to where they can make the greatest difference together.
- One major goal was to bring empowerment
to families in crisis by identifying the supplies needed
to close child welfare cases.
- These were families
that didn't have bad parents,
but simply poor parents, and didn't have basic necessities
like a bed or a bed frame or a dresser,
which is a thing that the city could actually remove a kid
out of the home.
- 2,000 families stepped forward to foster
or adopt children in need.
They also formed relationships
with over 5,000 experiencing homelessness,
connecting them with programs that led to housing.
- One of our convictions as a movement is
we're not gonna treat people like a problem to be solved,
instead we're gonna come around people
and bring relationship,
bring holistic community support
to individuals to bring true empowerment.
- You know, at the end of the day,
we're able to put on
probably the most outrageous humanitarian relief campaign
in a city where we had endless amounts
of critics that said, "That's impossible here."
- Love Has No Limits
is planning to launch similar campaigns
in more cities.
Their results in L.A. have shown the lasting impact
that can come from facilitating a network of relationships
between faith groups and local nonprofits.
- I think when we look across America
and look at our cities
and think about all the different entities
and roles community champions are playing,
we really see these as an ‘energy grid’
of resource, of services and solutions that can be provided.
You know, and I think faith communities
and NGOs are the greatest example of that.
And we realize that when you create a vision large enough
for anyone to be a part of it,
it allows there to be a lift for all ships,
all organizations.
- It makes it so sustainable when you build that way
that the people driving the initiatives long term
are fixtures in the city,
and they're gonna be there 5, 10, 15,
and even more years from now.
And as people continue to work collaboratively,
continue to work intelligently
on addressing challenges facing our communities,
there's actually impact happening.
It's measurable, it's evident all around us,
and we haven't even scratched the surface
of what's possible.
